[app-build-scripts] App Build Scripts (4 files, coh=0.00 coup=0.00)

  Application entry point, environment configuration, platform services, and the scheduler engine that drives bedtime enforcement at runtime.

<findings>

[warning] Non-Swift packaging assets are merged into the overlay UI zone by community detection, masking the true cohesion of the OverlayController/OverlayView/OverlayWindow triad. [overlay-ui]
[warning] Project configuration files (Makefile, LICENSE, .gitignore) are merged with core Swift domain models due to shared absence of import edges, obscuring the models' architectural role. [project-root-models]
[warning] All zone cohesion and coupling metrics are 0.0 with no detected cross-zone imports, indicating Swift import graph resolution was incomplete; treat zone membership as approximate until re-analyzed with full dependency data.
[warning] Universal 0-cohesion across all zones suggests the primary source language is not JavaScript/TypeScript (or the JS/TS entry points are minimal); consider supplementing sourcevision's import-graph analysis with file-proximity clustering or manifest-based dependency analysis to get meaningful architectural signal.
[warning] AppEnvironment is an undocumented composition root — it is the single point where all inter-service wiring happens, but no comment or type annotation marks it as such, increasing the risk that a future contributor adds service coupling elsewhere. [app-lifecycle]
[warning] No service protocols exist for SchedulerEngine or OverlayController — AppEnvironment couples concrete types directly, making the composition root difficult to test in isolation. [app-lifecycle]
[warning] The GoToBed app target has no corresponding test target — SchedulerEngine, OverlayController, AppEnvironment, and their inter-service wiring have zero automated test coverage. [core-domain-tests]
[warning] No automated enforcement of cross-zone layering exists: the architectural boundary between domain (project-root-models), UI (settings-ui, overlay-ui), and composition (app-lifecycle) is expressed only by file organization, not by Swift module visibility rules or CI checks — this boundary will erode silently as the codebase grows.
[warning] NSApp activation policy is switched inside OverlayController.present() as an undocumented side effect — callers have no way to opt out or observe the transition, creating hidden global state coupling. [overlay-ui]
[warning] The full inter-service event chain (Store → SchedulerEngine → AppEnvironment closure → OverlayController) is tested by no automated path; a regression in any link would only surface at runtime.
[warning] If settings-ui components directly mutate project-root-models types rather than delegating writes through a coordinator in app-lifecycle, that is a leaky abstraction: presentation layer is reaching past its intended boundary into the domain layer. [settings-ui]
[warning] No build step links static-site-assets content to the Swift source zones — llms.txt and sitemap.xml are manually authored, creating documentation drift risk on every feature release. [static-site-assets]
[warning] No zone boundary is machine-enforced between the three production UI zones (settings-ui, overlay-ui, app-lifecycle). Swift module visibility (internal vs. public) is the only available compile-time mechanism to prevent settings-ui from importing overlay-ui types or vice versa, but there is no evidence this is used intentionally as an architectural constraint — meaning cross-UI-zone coupling can accumulate invisibly.
[critical] The dependency direction app-lifecycle → settings-ui (to present the settings window via SettingsWindowController) combined with a potential settings-ui → project-root-models write path means settings-ui is coupled upward to the composition root and downward to the domain layer simultaneously. A pure UI zone should have exactly one dependency direction: receiving data from the domain and emitting user actions upward — not owning window lifecycle or writing domain state.
[warning] MenuContent.swift (the status-bar NSMenu) is co-located with settings views, making the app's primary navigation surface a subordinate member of a settings zone. Any future requirement to hide, modify, or gate the status-bar menu requires touching settings-ui rather than the composition root, which will surprise contributors. [settings-ui]
... +8 more

</findings>
